setting.note: Kazantzakis sets the novel on the coast of Crete, his own island, where the young narrator and the labourer Alexis Zorba reopen a lignite gallery and build a brief life by the sea. The real venture behind it happened across the water in the Messenian Mani. The map keeps both: it anchors the explicit Crete of the book at Heraklion, the author's birthplace, and marks the Peloponnesian mine that actually inspired it.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Nikos Kazantzakis, first published in Greek in 1946 as Vios kai Politeia tou Alexi Zorba. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Zorba the Greek, Nikos Kazantzakis (1946). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: Aegean blue for the sea the mine looks onto, shaded toward the black lignite the story is dug out of. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
